turns.content: It's been six months since my mom died and I should be over this by now. I'm drinking every night to get through it. Last night was a whole bottle of wine. I don't really care, honestly.

Six months, a bottle of wine, and "I don't really care."

What are you actually feeling right now, underneath the not caring?

*pause*

Nothing, mostly. That's kind of the point.

I mean — there's this low-level hum of something that I can't really name. It's not sadness exactly. It's more like... static. Like a TV that's been left on in another room. And the wine turns the volume down on it for a few hours, which is the only reason I'm still functional at work.

I'm not here because I think I have a drinking problem, just so we're clear. I'm here because I can't figure out how to actually grieve her. Like, I don't know how to start.
That static — is it there right now, as you're saying this?

*quiet for a second*

Yeah. Yeah, it's there.

It's kind of always there. I just... don't usually say that out loud.

*slight shift, more matter-of-fact*

It's fine though. I function. I go to work. I answer emails. I'm handling the estate stuff, which is its own whole nightmare — she didn't have a will, so there's probate, and my brother and I are barely talking because of it. So there's a lot of logistics to stay on top of. That keeps me pretty occupied during the day.

The nights are harder. That's when the static gets louder.
What happens when you let the static be loud?

*long pause*

I don't. That's why there's wine.

*short laugh, no real humor in it*

I tried once, maybe three weeks after she died. Just sat with it. And it wasn't — it wasn't like crying or whatever I expected. It was more like this... panic. Like the floor wasn't there. I didn't know what I was even feeling, just that it was too big and I was alone in my apartment and there was nobody to call at 11pm who actually wanted to hear it.

So I stopped trying that. The wine is more predictable.

*quieter*

My friends checked in for maybe the first month. Now they've moved on. Which is fine. I get it. Life keeps going.

That panic — the floor not being there — that's not a sign something went wrong when you tried. That's actually what the grief feels like when it starts to arrive.

You stopped right at the door.

*sits with that for a second*

Hm.

*not dismissive, but not quite accepting it either*

Maybe. Or maybe I stopped because I live alone and there's a limit to how much you can fall apart by yourself at 11 o'clock at night with nobody there. Like, that's not a therapeutic exercise, that's just... being in freefall with no net.

*pause*

She wasn't easy, my mom. It's not like it's just sadness. There's other stuff in there that I don't really want to look at. Which is probably part of why I don't open the door.

*quieter, almost to herself*

I don't know what I'd find.
What are you most afraid you'd find?

*long silence*

That I'm relieved. 

*very quiet*

She was... it was complicated. She needed a lot. For a long time. And the last two years especially were — I was doing a lot of managing. From a distance, which made it worse somehow. Always on call, always waiting for the next crisis.

And then she died and part of me just... exhaled. And I hate that. I hate that that's in there.

*pulls back a little, more clipped*

So yeah. I don't really want to sit alone in my apartment at 11pm and look at that. The wine is easier.

*beat*

I'm not an alcoholic. I want to be clear about that. It's a temporary thing. Until I figure out how to get through this.

The relief makes complete sense. Two years of always-on, waiting for the next crisis — of course something in you exhaled when it ended.

That's not a bad thing you found in there. That's just true.

*pause*

What do you hate about it?

*something shifts slightly, eyes down*

Because she was my mom. You're not supposed to feel relieved that your mom is dead. That's not — that's not what good people feel.

*pause*

And she didn't have anyone else, really. It was mostly me. So the relief feels like... like I'm saying I didn't want to do it. Like I resented her. Which I did, sometimes. And now she's gone and I can't — there's no fixing that now. There's no calling her and having some kind of conversation where it gets resolved.

*voice drops*

It's just done. And I felt relieved. And that's what I have to live with.

*snaps back a little*

See, this is why I don't open the door. Because now I've said that out loud and I don't know what to do with it.

*almost challenging*

So what do I do with it? Just sit with it sober at 11pm? Is that the answer?

You loved her. That's what the relief is. Two years of carrying her, always on call, always waiting — you could only do that because you loved her. The relief isn't proof you didn't want to. It's proof of how much you were holding.

*pause*

The resentment and the love were both real. They were both there at the same time. That's not a moral failure. That's just what it looks like to love someone who needed more than any one person could give.

*quieter*

What you can't fix now — the conversation that didn't happen — what would you have wanted her to know?

*very still*

That I was tired. 

*pause*

And that I didn't want to be. I wanted to be the person who just... showed up without keeping score. But I couldn't always do that. And I never told her that. I just kept showing up and being quietly resentful and she probably felt it and now—

*stops*

*longer pause, voice quieter*

I wanted her to know that I loved her anyway. Even when I was tired. Even when I was bad at showing it.

*silence*

*then, more carefully, like she's testing something*

Isn't this what the body is asking for, though? Like — the drinking. Doesn't the body know what it needs to get through something like this? Shouldn't I just trust that for now, while I'm in the middle of it?

payload.seed_rationale: Client presents unprocessed grief with "should be over this by now" (i-cant-get-over-this-loss), flat affect and anhedonia ("don't really care") suggesting depression (im-depressed), and daily substance use as avoidance mechanism indicating burnout/exhaustion (im-burned-out).
bundle_reasons: Directly maps the client's exact statement ("should be over this by now") and the drinking-as-symptom pattern; provides the diagnostic framework Joe needs for this moment.

Client's drinking-every-night pattern, flat affect ("I don't really care"), and six-month timeline fit the depression presentation; Joe needs this diagnostic framework to distinguish depression layers and identify the repressed anger likely driving the substance use.

decision.bundle_reason: Directly maps the client's exact statement ("should be over this by now") and the drinking-as-symptom pattern; provides the diagnostic framework Joe needs for this moment.
decision.follow_reason: Client shows addiction behavior (drinking nightly, whole bottle) alongside undergrieved loss; these edges address multi-emotion grief, shame-blocking, and pattern-locking—all likely at play here.

decision.bundle_reason: Client's drinking-every-night pattern, flat affect ("I don't really care"), and six-month timeline fit the depression presentation; Joe needs this diagnostic framework to distinguish depression layers and identify the repressed anger likely driving the substance use.
decision.follow_reason: Client's "I don't really care" and nightly drinking suggest Form 1 anger repression (inward); this edge will help Joe identify and work with the anger underneath the apparent apathy.

decision.bundle_reason: Burnout is about running hard on adrenaline; this client is collapsed in depression and self-medication, a different presentation requiring grief and anger work, not energy-management coaching.
decision.follow_reason: None of these edges fit: client isn't running on adrenaline, isn't managing energy, isn't in a post-rest trap—they're in acute grief-driven depression with substance use masking anger.

payload.seed_rationale: Client is grieving a death (mother), using wine to avoid the panic/helplessness underneath, experiencing isolation as friends have withdrawn, and managing through dissociation and busyness rather than feeling. The core presenting material is unprocessed grief with avoidance through substance and isolation.
bundle_reasons: Directly maps the client's situation: undergrieved loss (mother's death), avoidance through wine, panic when attempting to feel, and the "should be over this" framing that is locking the grief in place.

Client is isolating grief through wine and refusing to feel panic alone; the file maps the exact abandonment imprint ("nobody to call at 11pm who actually wanted to hear it") driving the avoidance pattern.

Client is avoiding helplessness (the panic when sitting with grief) through wine and control strategies; this principle directly maps the path from avoidance to empowerment through feeling the helplessness underneath.

decision.bundle_reason: Client is avoiding helplessness (the panic when sitting with grief) through wine and control strategies; this principle directly maps the path from avoidance to empowerment through feeling the helplessness underneath.

payload.seed_rationale: Client is grieving a mother who "wasn't easy," avoiding the full emotional experience of loss, and expressing fear about what emotions lie beneath the surface—guilt mixed with sadness, difficulty accessing and naming the full spectrum of feeling, and avoidance rooted in both isolation and the complexity of the relationship.
bundle_reasons: Directly maps the client's core dynamic: undergrieved loss, wine as avoidance, fear of what's underneath, and the shame/judgment shutting down the grief process.

Client is sitting with "other stuff in there" beneath the sadness about her mom—guilt, judgment, shame. This file maps exactly that structure: blockers on top, heartbreak-as-love underneath, and why she's defended against feeling it.
